text: This chapter teaches how dominant 7th chords and their alterations work within the cycle of 4ths/5ths, the organizing principle behind common-practice progressions. The master introduces augmented chords as dominant-derived (7#5 with no b7), establishes motion rules for dominants — 3-fret intervals for standard types, 2-fret intervals for altered dominants with 5ths and/or #5ths — and presents the m(maj7) chord as a half-step substitution for V7 function. Key techniques include the ii-V-I progression for smooth modulation to new keys, the 11b9 chord reserved for authentic V7 function, and the use of common tones in upper voices to ease transitions. Load-bearing terminology includes cycle of 4ths (also 5ths), V7 function (dominants as secondary dominants), modulation, and cycle-rooted chords in series, which form the framework for understanding how dominant chords create harmonic momentum and key changes in popular music.
